title: Chapter 1: The Commitment
content: <!-- Speed: Sprint | D4+D7 -->
The handcuffs were, Chloe decided, a bit much.

Not the metaphor — the actual steel ones. Her mother had clicked them around her wrists in the back seat of the Tesla, her expression the same one she'd worn at Chloe's seventh-grade piano recital: *disappointed, but not surprised*.
"For your own good," her mother said, not looking at her. "The Institute has a 94% success rate."

Chloe twisted her wrists. The cuffs were real police-grade, which meant her mother had either borrowed them from her new husband the district attorney, or — more likely — bought them on Amazon Prime. Same-day delivery. For her daughter.
"Success rate at what, exactly?" Chloe asked, her voice flat. "Producing Stepford wives?"
"My God, Chloe." Her mother's jaw tightened. "You can't even take *help* without making it into a joke."

The Tesla glided past Menlo Park, then up into the redwoods. Chloe watched the suburbs thin out, replaced by trees so old and thick they blocked the California sun like a cathedral ceiling. Somewhere in those woods was Serenity Wellness Institute — a name that came out of a marketing consultant's fever dream, all soft vowels and promises.

What it actually was, Chloe had learned in a three-hour research rabbit hole the night before her mother's "intervention": a high-end compliance factory. The kind of place where wealthy families sent their inconvenient daughters. The ones who quit law school to become artists. The ones who refused to marry the right business partner. The ones who posted too much on social media about systemic oppression and not enough about gratitude journals.
The ones who made the family look bad.
"You know they can't legally hold me, right?" Chloe said. "I'm twenty-three. I have rights. I'm not a minor, I'm not a danger to myself, I'm not — "
"You posted a TikTok," her mother cut in, voice ice-cold, "calling your father a 'venture capitalist who exploits human misery for quarterly returns.' It has four million views."
"Five point two, actually. The Duolingo account dueted it."
"His board saw it."
"Good."
Her mother's hands tightened on the steering wheel. Through the gap between the front seats, Chloe could see the tendons in her wrists cord like piano wire.
"You don't understand what you've done," her mother said quietly. "This isn't about you being *difficult*. This is about you being *dangerous*. To yourself. To this family."
"To the family's stock price."
Silence.

The car turned onto a private road, gravel crunching under tires that cost more than Chloe's entire Brooklyn apartment. A sign announced: *Serenity Wellness Institute — A Place for Growth and Renewal.* Behind it, a sprawling compound of cedar-and-glass buildings, all clean lines and organic architecture, the kind of place that looked like a spa in the marketing photos.
The kind of place you never left the same way you came in.
Two orderlies in white scrubs were already waiting at the entrance. Big men. Purposeful.
"I didn't sign anything," Chloe said.
"You don't have to." Her mother stepped out of the car. "It's a family referral. Your father and I both signed."
The orderly opened Chloe's door. The handcuffs came off, replaced immediately by a grip on her upper arm that was technically not painful but very, very clear.
"Chloe," her mother said, one hand on the Tesla's door, poised to leave. "Try. Just... try. For once in your life, stop *fighting* everything."

Chloe looked at her. Really looked. At the Botox, the subtle face-lift, the Lululemon jacket that cost more than rent. At the woman who had once, allegedly, been a person with dreams and opinions, before the money and the men and the maintenance had sanded her down into this: a delivery driver for her daughter's erasure.
"The thing is, Mom," Chloe said, letting the orderly guide her toward the building, "I actually like fighting. It's the one thing you never managed to beat out of me."

Her mother didn't answer. By the time Chloe looked back, the Tesla was already reversing down the gravel drive, taillights winking between the trees like the last two working pixels in a dying phone screen.

The automatic doors slid open. Inside, the air smelled like lavender and bleach. A woman in a dove-gray cardigan was waiting at the front desk, her smile calibrated to exactly the wattage that said *trust me* while meaning *obey me*.
"Welcome to Serenity," she said. "I'm Nurse Holloway. We're going to take very good care of you, Chloe."

Chloe looked at the woman's name tag. Looked at the cameras in every corner of the lobby. Looked at the brochure on the desk with its photos of women doing yoga, women journaling, women with the exact same vacant, peaceful expression.
Then she looked back at Nurse Holloway and smiled.
"Bestie," she said, "I have *so* many questions."
The smile on Nurse Holloway's face didn't move, but something behind it shifted. A micro-flinch. Chloe caught it like a scent on the air.
*Yeah*, she thought. *You've never had one like me before.*
The orderly's grip tightened on her arm. Chloe let it. Let them think she was just another angry girl they could break.
The thing none of them understood yet:

Chloe had spent her entire life being told she was broken. By parents, by partners, by professors with tenure and opinions. She'd been diagnosed, analyzed, medicated, therapized, and pathologized until the word *crazy* had stopped meaning anything at all.
At some point, she'd realized: if they were going to call you crazy no matter what you did...
...then you might as well do whatever you wanted.
The automatic doors closed behind her with a soft pneumatic hiss.
And Chloe, walking deeper into the institute with two hundred pounds of orderly on each arm, felt something she hadn't felt in years.
Anticipation.
This wasn't a prison.
It was a playground.
